title: 2024/12/12 - Petitions
displayTitle: 2024-12-12 - Petitions Committee - Petition of Paul Barton: Make Motor Neurone Disease a Notifiable Disease; Petition of Mark Baldwin: Remove certain prescribing restrictions for Nurs
topic: Petition of Paul Barton: Make Motor Neurone Disease a Notifiable Disease; Petition of Mark Baldwin: Remove certain prescribing restrictions for Nurs
description: 12 December 2024 | Petitions Committee Petition of Paul Barton: Make Motor Neurone Disease a Notifiable Disease; Petition of Mark Baldwin: Remove certain prescribing restrictions for Nurse Practitioners Petition of Paul Barton: Make Motor Neurone Disease a Notifiable Disease Paul Barton Ministry of Health: - Dr Nicholas Jones, Director of Public Health, Public Health Agency Petition of Mark Baldwin: Remove certain prescribing restrictions for Nurse Practitioners Mark Baldwin Nurse Practitioners New Zealand: - Chelsea Willmott, Chair NPNZ Medsafe: - Chris James, Group Manager - Matthew Spencer, Manager, Product Regulation - Susan Kenyon, Manager, Clinical Risk Management - Michael Haynes, Manager & Licensing Authority, Medicines Control
descriptionSummary: Petition of Paul Barton: Make Motor Neurone Disease a Notifiable Disease; Petition of Mark Baldwin: Remove certain prescribing restrictions for Nurs. Petition of Paul Barton: Make Motor Neurone Disease a Notifiable Disease; Petition of Mark Baldwin: Remove certain prescribing restrictions for Nurse...

chunks.text: Um, uh, you've, Greg and I have introduced ourselves. Let's get, uh, um, Carl, introduce yourself, Carl's member of And Ua. I'm a green MP TE called Papa, and I am the disability spokesperson for the Grains Yoda. I'm Barton. This is my wife Ally.

Um, Ali's unable to speak, so she'll be using a eye gaze computer, um, to talk with that. Alright. Well look, um, we've, uh, obviously had the, the, um, reports with this, but feel free, um, to make some submissions and, uh, available questions, hopefully.

Yeah, for Sure. So look forward to hearing from you. Um, so my full name is Paul Barton. Um, my wife was diagnosed with me neuro disease in 2021. Um, she was a nurse, ICU nurse. We were living in Sydney at the time. Um, and it started off in a right ring finger and then spread through her arm, into her leg.

Um, we moved over here. She's, um, Kiwi citizen. We moved over here in 2021, end of that year. Um, because Ally needed support. We weren't able to get support where we were living. Um, so I'm her full-time carer and that's my, that's my job through individualized funding and everything.

Um, so I'm looking after Ally. Unfortunately, she's unable to do anything for, for herself. Um, her speech kind of declined since 2022 and she's using a grid pad now in a high gaze. She can operate a wheelchair. Um, she can walk with my assistance or a walking frame, but she has to have the brakes on, so she just pushes it along.

That's how she gets around at home. Um, she's probably one of the youngest people with motor neuron disease in the entire country. Um, and the frustrating part of the illness is that most people are given three to five years and there's nothing that anybody can do. So whenever you ask a question we don't know is usually the answer.

Nobody knows what causes it. Um, there is one drug that's prescribed that may increase your life by three months, but is that three months walking around or is that three months when you're in a bed unable to do anything? Um, and I, I would accept, we don't know, um, if I felt that other measures were being taken or anything had been done.

You know, if, if people were trying everything they could, I would accept. We don't know. But, um, what I really noticed is that upon diagnosis, it's not legally required to be reported as a notifiable illness. So there's all this information that's being, uh, missed out on occupation, age, location, environment, all of these factors.

If there's a cluster of out of people getting diagnosed, why are people getting diagnosed at that place? And making it notifiable, I feel would be the beginning of understanding this illness because they can only work out how to treat it or what causes it if they can get the information.

Um, there's, the rebuttal I got was, it's not infectious, it's not a risk to public health, but that's why I wrote this paper outlining all of the links that have been, um, published with occupation. Um, if you live in an area of New South Wales where there's a high incidence of cyanobacteria in the water, the bluegreen algae mm-hmm.

Um, you are seven times more likely to get motor neuron disease. If you worked in farming and agriculture. The, the rate of incidence is higher. If you handle pesticides or ants, the rate is higher. What we believe caused Ali to reach a tipping point and have this cascade failure was we needed our bedroom fumigated three times in six weeks for bed bugs.

They stowed away on a backpack from a hotel. Um, and then, you know, a couple of months after that, she started with the tingling in her finger. But there's so many people being diagnosed with so many different lifestyles or ways of, um, occupations or things like that is hard to narrow down what the common factors could be.

And I feel that making it notifiable would be the start of figuring out what to do. Alright, well thank you. Um, and that's, so got nothing else to add, so any questions? Member served debates. Thank you, chair. Thank you for, uh, the presentation and for the paper.

And what I'm, uh, struck by in a big way is the facts that what you are seeking to do is not something to solve any, your own charge, but to support, um, the, the prevention of, of, uh, this disease go forward and that, and that I, um, am feeling quite strongly.

Um, have you been given any, uh, direction at all about other ways that the outcome of ensuring the information collection could occur, um, could be achieved if, if there been any? Um, There is an m, m and D registry, but that is not, um, it's optional.

So the diagnosing physician is not legally obliged to answer, answer anything. Um, that comes from, you might be approached by somebody that says, oh, have you thought about registering? And then you go, probably, I'll think about it. You're not rushing out to do that 'cause you've got enough on your plate.

You've got to go through those five stages of acceptance and everything. Um, and you've got so much on your, on your mind. I mean, we don't have any children, but there's people that are older than Ally that have this illness and they've got families and all this kind of thing. Mortgages, car payments, you can't work.

We'd, we'd love to be working. Ali is a dedicated nurse, but unfortunately that's not the case. So, um, the only thing I could think would be to make the m and d registry opt out and upon diagnosis, you're automatically registered would be a start.

And as I outlined in my paper, possibly the MND registry could be changed and the questions that they asked that I included in the appendices, it could be tailored more to, to collect the information that would be, um, gathered if it was made notifiable.

Um, talking with, um, doctor at Massey University and, and the CEO of the Mindy New Zealand, things that, that could be asked would be where did you grow up? Mm-hmm. Um, what age did you first get symptoms? What was your occupation? Where did you live 30 years ago?

Where did you live 20 years ago? Where did you live 10 years ago? Um, and it seems like trivial things, but it's about narrowing it down and mapping everything out. And that's the only way I think we're gonna figure out what causes it. Only 10% is genetic and familial. And there is a drug called tosin that's just been approved, just been approved over here.

Tosin Ali doesn't have that gene mutation. There's one family in the West coast and they've had 25 plus family members all having m and d and it's from this gene mutation. And the gentleman went to Canada in order to get this drug. Um, and I think he's moved, he's moved back now, I believe he is in the Greymouth area and the tofa and is working and he is able to be working again and getting his life back.

But for Ali, that's not an option. And to be honest, people with this illness just feel like they're not being heard and they've been given this diagnosis, diagnosis, which is awful. And then they just feel like nobody's listening to them go over there and just succumb quietly is the harsh reality I think that people are dealing with.

Can I ask a question? Are you aware Of any country in the world where it is is notifiable? It is not notifiable in any country at all. Um, thank you so much for this petition. So, uh, my name's Nicholas Jones. I'm the director of public health, uh, currently working in the Ministry of Health. And I'm here today to speak to our response to the petition. Um, firstly, I just want to acknowledge the importance of this condition.

And as, um, Mr. Barton has very adequately and eloquently pointed out the importance of this condition, uh, particularly with the potential linkage to to chemical exposures. Um, we agree, um, we agree that this is a very important issue and, um, we also agree with the need, uh, to do, to gather the required information to be able to try and identify what the causes of this condition are, identify clusters, all, all of the objectives actually that have been raised in the petition.

Um, at this stage, the ministry's view is that we don't support it being made a notifiable disease. Um, but having said that, um, we are very interested in working with the, um, registry to see if there is a way for that registry to be improved in such a way that it would, uh, collect the required information that would be required to help address these questions.

Um, on the matter of, uh, whether it should be made mandatory or not, um, there isn't a legislative basis at the moment for, um, that registration to be made mandatory, but I would support, um, the idea, um, you know, potentially of considering an opt off at the moment.

There isn't a way for us easily to do that. Uh, I guess the question would be, the counter argument would be, um, for, uh, a an effective person who really didn't want to share their information, um, with the registry, um, do we collectively think that they should be compelled to do so?

Uh, and that's probably a question that I think best sits, um, with, you know, my colleagues there on the select committee and in parliament. Um, as to what is the strength of the argument for making out a compulsory registration with the, um, with the register? Um, there is some work going on.

I think that it's helpful for the committee to be aware of. Um, we are formally reviewing our criteria for making notifiable diseases notifiable under the Health Act. And, um, we're early in the phase of this, uh, this piece of work. We will be consulting more widely on the criteria, as has been discussed.

Uh, typically the criteria are primarily around the need for there to be some public health action taken as a result of the occurrence of the illness. And typically this is in relation to an infectious disease, but not always. There could be, uh, non-infectious diseases where, um, for example, lead poisoning is a good example where there is a need for an urgent, uh, public health action to ensure that say, children who have elevated levels of lead are not being exposed to further, um, contamination with this particular disease.

Uh, and they're likely to be, you know, a long latency between when the person is initially exposed to whatever cause and when they start to develop symptoms. Um, it's quite difficult to make the argument that there is a need to act on every individual case and to conduct a detailed case follow up.

Um, where, where I would agree, uh, with Mr. Barton is that there is a very solid argument for ensuring that information is collected about cases. So that the big picture in terms of what's going on in New Zealand and what are the kind of things that might be contributing, are collected, that information is collected.

Um, we have offered to work with the, um, register and, and with Mr. Barton, uh, to see if there are ways in which that can be expanded. Uh, I am, um, not sure of the way in which that register is currently funded, so there may be issues that be need to be worked through in terms of, um, the contractual relationship that they have and what the expectations are In terms of, um, the expectations for that, for that registry, it's primarily, it would appear focused on collecting, inform clinical information, so information about the person's, uh, clinical state, uh,

their state, their needs, and potentially facilitating their access to trials. Um, as has been mentioned, the po potential for there to be, um, you know, early trials and people who are registered would then be eligible to be able to access those. But I'll probably leave it there and, uh, open open to taking questions.

Alright. Uh, Mr. Bates, Thanks, chair. I've got a couple of questions if you don't mind. Um, thank you for the, the contribution there. A couple of questions, firstly around the notifiable, um, case. Is the criteria and the reason for, um, this not being able to be notifiable predominantly driven by the criteria?

Um, or is it a policy choice that Health New Zealand get to make? If you understand what I'm asking. Um, the, the, the choice actually is not, the policy is not set by Health New Zealand. It's, it's actually set by the Ministry of Health, um, sorry, The Ministry of Health. Yeah, Yeah. Um, so the, I mean, the essential, the balancing act is to balance the, um, benefits in terms of immediate actions with, um, both the, the costs of, uh, the service in terms of where we, where we deploy the public health service

and the medical offices of Health who receive these, um, these notifications. Um, because they are receiving many notifications for many different diseases. So we, we have to also be cognizant of that resource and how it's deployed. Uh, and clearly when there are immediate benefits such as ensuring, um, the contacts of a case of infectious disease are given some kind of intervention to prevent it spreading, there is an urgency and, and immediacy to the, you know, the rationale for making that a, a, um, notifiable disease.

But essentially the, the criteria at a high level are about whether or not there is an individual case response required. Um, then there is, um, the significance of a prevention program. So if we have a, for example, a, uh, an immunization program that is designed to try and prevent diseases, then it is very useful to make it notifiable to be ensuring whether we're actually being effective in terms of our prevention activities.

Um, then obviously we do take into account, um, whether a particular population groups, um, are more effective than others. And, you know, in a lot of conditions we do see Maori and Pacific people being more effective than others. And there is a strong argument for us having a good way to ensure that we are actually working towards equity in terms of outcomes.

And then, um, other subpopulations, um, you know, we, we are conscious that there may be other populations, disability populations, for example, and obviously this is one case where disability is, is important here, um, where we would be considering that as a criteria. Um, and then there are emerging conditions, um, or reemerging conditions where we need to be collecting better information to be able to assess, um, what's going on in terms of trying to identify, um, the, um, likely causes.

Um, clearly anything which is causing an outbreak, um, is a priority, um, because of the need to sort of take wider outbreak activity responses. So that's the sort of general area of criteria.
